summary: Gruber defends Apple against criticism that Snow Leopard shipped with an outdated version of Flash Player containing security vulnerabilities. He argues that Adobe released the Flash update only eight days before Snow Leopard went GM, making it unreasonable to expect Apple to swap in an untested component at the last minute. He also addresses the related complaint that Snow Leopard's installer overwrites user-installed newer versions of Flash, explaining this is standard installer behavior for all system components. The post frames the controversy as overblown and implicitly questions why Apple should bear responsibility for Adobe's software quality.
key_points: Adobe released the Flash security update only eight days before Snow Leopard went GM, making last-minute inclusion impractical
Apple's installer overwrites all system components with tested versions, not just Flash — this is standard behavior, not a Flash-specific decision
Replacing 'the single-crashiest piece of software in Mac OS X' with an untested version right before GM would be reckless
The framing implies Adobe shouldn't get to dictate Apple's release schedule through poorly timed updates
money_quotes: Does anyone really think that Apple should have replaced the single-crashiest piece of software in Mac OS X with a new untested version just eight days before going GM?
Should Apple allow Adobe to set the schedule for Mac OS X updates?
The installer gives you an entire system that has been tested and whose components are known to work as expected, and Flash, whether we like it or not, is part of the system.
Should Apple have postponed Snow Leopard for another month?
key_insight: Apple shipping an older version of Flash with Snow Leopard was a reasonable engineering decision driven by release timelines, not negligence, and the real problem is Flash's chronic instability.
